text: The surgeon begins by carefully dissecting omental adhesions from the edge of the liver, using surgical instruments to separate the adhered tissue.
The surgeon uses ICG fluorescence to assist in identifying structures while continuing to dissect the omentum from the liver edge, with the gallbladder not yet visible.
The surgeon works on the medial aspect of the gallbladder, progressing towards the fundus and moving to the lateral side.
The surgeon inadvertently enters the gallbladder, causing necrotic purulent material with fibrinous texture to emanate from its lumen.
The surgeon continues to remove the omentum from the gallbladder, gradually freeing it from adhesions until the omentum is completely detached.
The surgeon removes the drain to achieve proper retraction of the liver, taking down numerous adhesions above the liver to enhance exposure.
The surgeon observes that the cystic duct is not illuminated, while the common duct is visible. The peritoneum is being opened on either side of the suspected cystic duct using surgical instruments.
The surgeon attempts to focus on the cystic duct to achieve a normal critical view, despite the cystic duct not lighting up with ICG due to obstruction, making the dissection visually challenging.

The surgeon encounters a small vessel while attempting to access the cystic duct. The common bile duct is visible, positioned down and away from the surgical field, but the cystic duct remains difficult to access.
The surgeon begins to visualize the junction between the gallbladder and the cystic duct. The gallbladder is being separated from the liver bed to achieve the critical view of safety.
The surgeon identifies additional vessels near the suspected cystic duct, carefully navigating the area to confirm the duct's location.
The surgeon creates a window behind the suspected cystic duct but refrains from proceeding further due to the inability to confirm its identity with ICG lighting and the lack of a critical view.

The surgeon attempts to peel the posterior surface of the gallbladder from the liver bed to achieve a critical view. Due to difficulty, the surgeon adjusts the approach by moving higher on the gallbladder to access the area behind it, while the cystic artery remains unidentified.
The surgeon transects the gallbladder approximately one-third of the way up, attempting to visualize the lumen. However, the bottom half of the gallbladder is observed to lack a lumen entirely.
The surgeon observes a solid structure and identifies what appears to be the artery, positioned higher up on the medial aspect of the surgical field.
The surgeon attempts a modified top-down approach by dissecting behind the bottom third of the gallbladder, aiming to identify the cystic duct more clearly.

The surgeon carefully navigates around crossing vessels superiorly, attempting to identify the cystic duct amidst surrounding tissue. The area contains additional structures, and the surgeon cautiously examines to ensure no other anatomical elements are involved.

The surgeon divides the gallbladder above the previously transected area, compressing what is believed to be the cystic duct. Mucus is observed returning from the duct, confirming its identification, and the duct is clipped.
The surgeon targets the artery and divides it, then proceeds to remove the remaining portion of the gallbladder from the liver bed.
The surgeon maneuvers back and forth around the fibrotic capsule of the gallbladder, carefully detaching the remaining attachments from the liver bed, completing the dissection.
The surgeon irrigates the surgical field, ensuring hemostasis is achieved, with visible attention to preventing any bleeding complications.
A drain is visible in the surgical field. The common bile duct is identified, with the presumed cystic duct located beneath it. The surgical area appears intact, indicating no visible injury.
